youtube_items.summary: [DESCRIPTION FALLBACK] Mashable's Rachel Kraus walks through the many ways the digital divide has intensified during the COVID-19 pandemic — and ...

analysis.problem: Digital inequality persists, with nearly half of the world still offline, and the digital divide intensifying during the COVID-19 pandemic
analysis.root_cause: Lack of access to affordable and reliable internet infrastructure, particularly in rural and underprivileged areas
leverage_points: Telecommunication companies and internet service providers

proposed_actions.action: Implement low-cost, satellite-based internet services, such as those offered by Viasat
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Increase the number of people with access to reliable internet by 20% within the next 2 years

proposed_actions.action: Develop and deploy community-based internet infrastructure, such as mesh networks
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Reduce the digital divide by 15% in targeted areas within the next 3 years

evidence: Nearly half of the world is still offline, meaning half of the world is still unable to access the internet
The digital divide has intensified during the COVID-19 pandemic, with many people unable to access online education, healthcare, and other essential services
analysis.generalization: This problem connects to other domains, such as education, healthcare, and economic development, as access to reliable internet is essential for participating in the modern economy and accessing essential services
analysis.summary: The digital divide persists, with many people lacking access to reliable internet, and targeted actions are needed to address this issue and promote digital equality. Telecommunication companies and government agencies must work together to implement affordable and reliable internet infrastructure, particularly in rural and underprivileged areas.
